description: Persistent accept-list of editorially-verified scanner false positives for the book/tools/audit/ pipeline. Pass 16 Phase 2 added the 5 abbreviation-first-use entries below; these are NOT mechanical FPs from a detector bug but semantic/editorial decisions where the scanner's canonical §10.5 expansion would produce factually wrong text (IR=infrared vs. intermediate-rep), break proper nouns (RISC-V), lose author phrasing parallelism (matrix-matrix GEMM vs. GEMV), or distort §9-protected callout structural labels (SLA bold bullet). Every entry must be justified by a specific clause of book-prose-merged.md and each carries a full justification string for future maintainers.

entries.before: [^fn-faceid-privacy]: **Face ID**: Apple's biometric system projects 30,000 IR dots for 3D face mapping, processed entirely within the Secure Enclave, an isolated cryptographic coprocessor whose memory is inaccessible even to the main OS. Biometric templates never leave the device. This architecture achieves a 1:1,000,000 false acceptance rate while eliminating the network transmission that would otherwise create both a latency penalty and a data breach surface, illustrating that on-device constraints can simultaneously strengthen privacy and improve accuracy. \index{Face ID!privacy architecture}
entries.justification: IR here means infrared (Apple Face ID 30,000 IR dots for 3D face mapping), not 'intermediate representation'. The canonical §10.5 expansion 'intermediate representation (IR)' would be factually wrong in this context.

entries.before: [^fn-tinyml-compression-stack]: **TinyML Ecosystem Fragmentation**: Unlike cloud or mobile ML, where PyTorch or TensorFlow Lite provide a single optimization path, TinyML spans dozens of incompatible microcontroller families (ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V, Xtensa), each with different instruction sets, memory layouts, and vendor-specific toolchains. A model optimized for one target often requires re-quantization and re-validation for another, multiplying the engineering cost of multi-device deployment and creating portability barriers absent from higher-resource paradigms. \index{TinyML!ecosystem fragmentation}
entries.justification: RISC here is part of the 'RISC-V' ISA proper noun (Unlike PyTorch... TinyML Ecosystem Fragmentation discusses RISC-V toolchains). The canonical §10.5 expansion 'reduced instruction set computer (RISC)' is not appropriate when RISC is the first half of a proper-noun compound.

entries.before: The core computation is dense matrix-vector multiplication (GEMV) or matrix-matrix multiplication (GEMM) when batched. While regular and parallelizable, the arithmetic intensity (FLOPs/byte) is low for small batch sizes (the **batch size**\index{Batch Size} is the number of input samples processed together in one forward pass; larger batches amortize weight-loading cost over more computations). Modern processors optimize this via specialized SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) units (e.g., AVX-512 on CPUs) or systolic arrays (on Tensor Processing Units (TPUs)/GPUs) that amortize control overhead over massive blocks of parallel arithmetic.
entries.justification: Author's explicit parallel phrasing 'dense matrix-vector multiplication (GEMV) or matrix-matrix multiplication (GEMM)' preserves the GEMV/GEMM symmetry in the surrounding discussion. The canonical §10.5 expansion 'general matrix multiply (GEMM)' would break the parallelism with GEMV, which has no canonical expansion in §10.5. The author's synonym is editorially valid per §10.5's intent (introduce the abbreviation on first use), and the scanner's regex does not recognize 'matrix-matrix multiplication' as an acceptable alternate form.

entries.before: Teams design on-device learning systems assuming uniform hardware capabilities across deployment devices. Real-world deployments span diverse hardware with varying computational power, memory capacity, energy constraints, and networking capabilities. Edge device capabilities span 6+ orders of magnitude: from 32 KB RAM microcontrollers to 16 GB smartphones, 48MHz ARM Cortex-M0+ (~10 MIPS) to 3GHz A-series processors (~100,000 MIPS), and 10μW sensor nodes to 5 W flagship phones. A learning algorithm that works well on high-end smartphones may fail catastrophically on resource-constrained IoT devices. Federated learning algorithms must dynamically adapt through quantized inference on low-end devices, selective participation based on capability, and tiered aggregation strategies that account for 10,000$\times$ performance differences within a single deployment.
entries.justification: MIPS here means 'million instructions per second' (context: '~10 MIPS … ~100,000 MIPS' hardware performance range), not the canonical §10.5 expansion 'microprocessor without interlocked pipelined stages (MIPS)', which is the RISC ISA family. Expanding to the ISA name would be factually wrong.

entries.before: - **SLA requirement**: Training must complete within 2 weeks
entries.justification: SLA appears inside a bold structural label `**SLA requirement**:` which is the first item of a parallel bullet list (`**SLA requirement**`, `**Current trajectory**`, `**Budget impact**`). Per §9 Protected Content, bold structural labels inside callouts are protected. Expanding the label to '**service level agreement (SLA) requirement**' would also break the alphabetical-parallel structure of the bullet list.
